content.text: Marketplace Managed Applications

A Marketplace managed application is the public-facing variant of Azure managed applications, distributed through the Azure Marketplace. An independent software vendor (ISV) publishes the application as a Marketplace offer; customers discover and deploy it through the Marketplace or Azure Portal. The ISV retains management access to the resources deployed in the customer's subscription via a managed resource group with a publisher-controlled lock.

This model is distinct from Service Catalog managed applications, which are internal to an organization. Marketplace managed apps are commercial products that appear in the public Azure Marketplace catalog and can include per-use or flat-fee billing through the customer's Azure invoice.

attrs.alt: Azure Marketplace managed application three-party model

content.text: Marketplace vs Service Catalog
Aspect
Service Catalog
Marketplace
Publisher
Internal IT / platform team
ISV or third-party vendor
Distribution
Within a single Azure tenant
Public Azure Marketplace, available globally
Billing
No Marketplace billing; pay only Azure resource costs
Optional Marketplace billing (per-use, flat fee) added to Azure invoice
Access control
AAD group or SP specified by publisher
ISV's AAD tenant principals specified in offer
Version management
Publisher updates definition resource
ISV publishes new plan versions through Partner Center
Consumer discovery
Portal > Service Catalog or CLI
Portal > Marketplace or direct offer link
Offer Structure in Partner Center

An ISV creates a Marketplace managed application offer in Microsoft Partner Center. The offer contains one or more plans; each plan has a package (.zip), pricing tier, and market availability settings. Microsoft reviews the offer before it becomes publicly available.
Offer type: Azure Application (not Virtual Machine or Container)
Plan type: Managed Application (as opposed to Solution Template, which does not use managed resource groups)
Package: same .zip format as Service Catalog — mainTemplate.json + createUiDefinition.json
Authorizations: ISV's own AAD group/SP gets Owner or Contributor on the customer's managed resource group
Notification webhook: optional HTTP endpoint ARM calls when the customer deploys, updates, or deletes the application

Partner Center submission requires a valid Microsoft Partner Network (MPN) account and passes through a technical review where Microsoft validates the package structure and UI definition. Allow 2-5 business days for initial approval.
Customer Deployment Experience

From the customer's perspective, a Marketplace managed application deployment is triggered through the Azure Portal Marketplace, the az marketplaceordering and az managedapp CLI commands, or an ARM template.

# 1. Accept the Marketplace offer terms (required once per subscription)
az vm image accept-terms \
  --publisher contoso \
  --offer contoso-web-platform \
  --plan standard-v2

# 2. Get the offer plan details
az managedapp list-definitions \
  --query '[?kind==`Marketplace`].[name,location]' -o table

# 3. Deploy the managed application
az managedapp create \
  --name my-contoso-app \
  --resource-group consumer-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --kind MarketPlace \
  --plan-name standard-v2 \
  --plan-product contoso-web-platform \
  --plan-publisher contoso \
  --plan-version '2.0.0' \
  --managed-rg-id '/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/my-contoso-app-mrg' \
  --parameters '{"appName": {"value": "myapp"}}'
ISV Access to the Managed Resource Group

After a customer deploys the application, the ISV's AAD principal (from the authorizations list in the offer) has the specified role on the managed resource group. This lets the ISV deploy updates, run diagnostics, and enforce compliance without requiring the customer to grant access manually.

# ISV: check current state of a customer's managed resource group
# (ISV runs this against their own service principal, cross-tenant)
az resource list \
  --resource-group customer-managed-rg \
  --query '[].{type:type,name:name,state:properties.provisioningState}' \
  -o table

# ISV: push an update to the managed resources
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group customer-managed-rg \
  --template-file updated-main.json \
  --parameters appName=customer-app appVersion=2.1.0
Just-In-Time (JIT) Access

JIT access is an optional feature that restricts the ISV's standing access to the managed resource group. Instead of permanent Owner access, the ISV's principals have no role by default and must request time-limited access for specific maintenance operations. The customer approves or denies the request.
JIT access is configured in the Marketplace offer's plan settings in Partner Center.
The ISV calls the JIT access API to request elevated access for a specified duration (1-8 hours).
The customer receives a notification and approves or denies the request through the Azure Portal or the Microsoft.Solutions/jitRequests REST API.
Access is granted automatically after customer approval, then revoked at the end of the duration.
All JIT access requests are recorded in the Activity Log on the managed resource group for auditability.

# Customer: list pending JIT requests for a managed application
az rest --method get \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/<consumerRG>/providers/Microsoft.Solutions/applications/<appName>/jitRequests?api-version=2021-07-01'

# Customer: approve a JIT request
az rest --method patch \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/<consumerRG>/providers/Microsoft.Solutions/applications/<appName>/jitRequests/<requestName>?api-version=2021-07-01' \
  --body '{"properties": {"jitRequestState": "Approved"}}'
Notification Webhooks

The ISV can register a webhook URL in the Marketplace offer. ARM calls this URL whenever a customer creates, updates, or deletes the managed application. The webhook payload contains the customer's subscription ID, managed resource group ID, and application parameters — allowing the ISV's backend to provision licenses, send welcome emails, or trigger onboarding workflows.
Lifecycle Management for Customers

# List all Marketplace managed applications in a subscription
az managedapp list \
  --query '[?kind==`MarketPlace`].{name:name,rg:resourceGroup,state:properties.provisioningState}' \
  -o table

# Show a specific application and its managed resource group
az managedapp show \
  --name my-contoso-app \
  --resource-group consumer-rg

# Delete the managed application
# This also deletes the managed resource group (and all its resources)
az managedapp delete \
  --name my-contoso-app \
  --resource-group consumer-rg

# Verify the managed resource group was also removed
az group show --name my-contoso-app-mrg 2>&1 | grep -q 'not found' && echo 'Managed RG deleted'

Deleting a Marketplace managed application immediately triggers an ARM webhook call to the ISV endpoint (if configured) and removes the managed resource group. This is irreversible — ensure any stateful data (database exports, backups) is preserved before deletion.
Python SDK — Querying and Managing Marketplace Apps
The ApplicationClient in the Azure SDK for Python supports full lifecycle management of Marketplace managed applications programmatically.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource.managedapplications import ApplicationClient
from azure.mgmt.resource.managedapplications.models import (
    Application, ApplicationProperties, Plan
)

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ApplicationClient(credential, subscription_id)

# List all managed applications in a resource group
apps = client.applications.list_by_resource_group('consumer-rg')
for app in apps:
    kind = app.kind
    state = app.properties.provisioning_state
    mrg = app.properties.managed_resource_group_id
    print(f'{app.name} ({kind}) — {state}')
    print(f'  Managed RG: {mrg}')

# List all managed applications across the subscription
all_apps = client.applications.list_by_subscription()
marketplace_apps = [
    a for a in all_apps
    if a.kind and a.kind.lower() == 'marketplace'
]
print(f'Marketplace managed apps in subscription: {len(marketplace_apps)}')
Creating a Marketplace App via SDK

# Deploy a Marketplace managed application
poller = client.applications.begin_create_or_update(
    resource_group_name='consumer-rg',
    application_name='my-contoso-app',
    parameters=Application(
        location='eastus',
        kind='MarketPlace',
        plan=Plan(
            name='standard-v2',
            product='contoso-web-platform',
            publisher='contoso',
            version='2.0.0',
        ),
        properties=ApplicationProperties(
            managed_resource_group_id=(
                f'/subscriptions/{subscription_id}'
                '/resourceGroups/my-contoso-app-mrg'
            ),
            parameters={
                'appName': {'value': 'myapp'},
            },
        ),
    ),
)
result = poller.result()
print(f'Deployed: {result.id}')
ISV: Building a Notification Webhook Handler

The notification webhook lets the ISV's backend react to customer lifecycle events. A minimal webhook handler needs to validate the incoming request and process the event type (PUT for create/update, DELETE for removal).

# Flask webhook handler for ARM notifications
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
import hmac, hashlib

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/arm-webhook', methods=['POST'])
def arm_webhook():
    payload = request.get_json()
    event_type = payload.get('eventType')  # 'PUT' or 'DELETE'
    app_id = payload.get('applicationId', '')
    state = payload.get('provisioningState')

    # Extract customer subscription ID from the application resource ID
    # Format: /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/...
    parts = app_id.split('/')
    customer_sub = parts[2] if len(parts) > 2 else 'unknown'

    if event_type == 'PUT' and state == 'Succeeded':
        # New customer deployed — provision license, send welcome email
        provision_customer_license(customer_sub, app_id)

    elif event_type == 'DELETE':
        # Customer deleted — revoke license, clean up external resources
        revoke_customer_license(customer_sub)

    # ARM requires a 200 OK response within 10 seconds
    return jsonify({'status': 'received'}), 200

def provision_customer_license(sub_id, app_id):
    print(f'Provisioning license for subscription {sub_id}')

def revoke_customer_license(sub_id):
    print(f'Revoking license for subscription {sub_id}')

ARM expects a 200 OK from the webhook within 10 seconds. For slow operations (license provisioning, email), respond immediately and process the event asynchronously using a queue (Azure Service Bus, Storage Queue).
Controlling Managed Resource Group Visibility

By default, customers can see resources in the managed resource group but cannot modify them. ISVs can further control visibility by setting the customerActions property in the ARM template output, or by using Azure deny assignments to prevent specific resource types from being read.
Common Issues and Resolutions
Issue
Cause
Resolution
Offer terms not accepted
Customer's subscription hasn't accepted the Marketplace terms
Run az vm image accept-terms with the publisher/offer/plan before deploying via CLI or template
InvalidPlan error on create
Plan name, product, or publisher string mismatch
Use az vm image list --publisher <pub> to get exact values
Webhook not called on deployment
Webhook URL returns non-200 or times out (>10s)
Return 200 immediately; process events asynchronously via a queue
ISV cannot access managed RG
Cross-tenant access requires AAD B2B or the ISV's SP must be in the customer's tenant
ISV uses the ARM API cross-tenant endpoint with their own credentials (ARM handles the cross-tenant RBAC)
JIT request not visible to customer
JIT access not enabled in the Partner Center plan settings
Enable JIT access in the plan configuration before publishing the offer version
Enterprise Procurement and Azure Marketplace Private Offers

Large enterprises often restrict which Marketplace offers employees can deploy. Private Offers allow an ISV to negotiate custom pricing with a specific customer, making the offer available only to that customer's Azure account. Marketplace Private Plans restrict offer visibility to a specific set of Azure subscription IDs.

# Enterprise admin: view available Marketplace offers restricted by Azure Policy
az policy definition list \
  --query '[?displayName.contains(@, `Marketplace`)].{name:displayName,id:id}' \
  -o table

# Check if a specific Marketplace product is allowed in the current tenant
# (This query checks if Azure Policy blocks the deployment)
az policy state list \
  --resource '/subscriptions/<subId>' \
  --query '[?complianceState==`NonCompliant` && contains(policyDefinitionName, `marketplace`)].resourceId' \
  -o table

# List private offers available to this subscription
az rest --method get \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<subId>/providers/Microsoft.Marketplace/privateStores/default/offers?api-version=2023-01-01' \
  --query 'value[].{name:name,publisher:properties.publisherId}' \
  -o table
Deploying a Private Offer via ARM Template
Auditing Marketplace App Usage with Resource Graph

Security and compliance teams can use Azure Resource Graph to inventory all Marketplace managed applications across the tenant, including which ISV plans are in use and the provisioning state of each instance.

# Inventory all Marketplace managed apps across all subscriptions
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.solutions/applications'
  | where kind =~ 'marketplace'
  | extend publisher = tostring(plan.publisher)
  | extend product = tostring(plan.product)
  | extend planName = tostring(plan.name)
  | extend planVersion = tostring(plan.version)
  | project subscriptionId, resourceGroup, name,
      publisher, product, planName, planVersion,
      state = properties.provisioningState
  | order by publisher asc
" --subscriptions $(az account list --query '[].id' -o tsv | tr '\n' ' ') \
  -o table 2>/dev/null || \
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.solutions/applications'
  | where kind =~ 'marketplace'
  | project subscriptionId, resourceGroup, name,
      publisher = plan.publisher, product = plan.product,
      state = properties.provisioningState
" -o table

Cross-subscription Resource Graph queries require the 'Management Group Contributor' or equivalent role at the scope you want to query. For tenant-wide queries, run against a management group that contains all target subscriptions.
Summary

Marketplace managed applications extend the Service Catalog model to public commercial distribution through the Azure Marketplace. ISVs publish versioned offer plans through Partner Center; customers deploy through the Marketplace UI or CLI with the kind: MarketPlace flag. The ISV retains management access via authorizations, can optionally require JIT approval for elevated access, and receives lifecycle notifications via webhooks. Customers are billed through their Azure subscription, consolidating Marketplace software fees with Azure resource costs.
